title: Probate court

(sometimes called a **surrogate court**, **orphans' court**,[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) or **court of ordinary**) is a [court](/wiki/Court "Court") that has competence in a [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") to deal with matters of [probate](/wiki/Probate "Probate") and the [administration of estates](/wiki/Administration_of_an_estate_on_death "Administration of an estate on death").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In some jurisdictions probate court functions are performed by a [chancery court](/wiki/Chancery_court "Chancery court") or another [court of equity](/wiki/Court_of_equity "Court of equity"), or as a part or division of another court.
is an elected position in jurisdictions such as Maryland.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Court dealing with probate and estates

"Court of Ordinary" redirects here. For courts with general jurisdiction, see [ordinary court](/wiki/Ordinary_court "Ordinary court").

|  |  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | **This article has multiple issues.** Please help **[improve it](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Probate_court "Special:EditPage/Probate court")** or discuss these issues on the **[talk page](/wiki/Talk:Probate_court "Talk:Probate court")**. *([Learn how and when to remove these messages](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  | | --- | --- | | [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This article **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Probate_court "Special:EditPage/Probate court") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["Probate court"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Probate+court%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Probate+court%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Probate+court%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Probate+court%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Probate+court%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Probate+court%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(October 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  |  |  | | --- | --- | | Globe icon. | The examples and perspective in this article **deal primarily with USA and common law jurisdictions and do not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Probate_court "Talk:Probate court"), or [create a new article](/wiki/Wikipedia:Article_wizard "Wikipedia:Article wizard"), as appropriate. *(December 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |  *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg/40px-Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg)

Look up ***[probate court](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/probate_court "wiktionary:probate court")*** in Wiktionary, the free dictionary.

A **probate court** (sometimes called a **surrogate court**, **orphans' court**,[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) or **court of ordinary**) is a [court](/wiki/Court "Court") that has competence in a [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") to deal with matters of [probate](/wiki/Probate "Probate") and the [administration of estates](/wiki/Administration_of_an_estate_on_death "Administration of an estate on death").[[2]](#cite_note-2) In some jurisdictions probate court functions are performed by a [chancery court](/wiki/Chancery_court "Chancery court") or another [court of equity](/wiki/Court_of_equity "Court of equity"), or as a part or division of another court.

Probate courts administer proper distribution of the [assets](/wiki/Asset "Asset") of a [decedent](/wiki/Death "Death") (one who has died), [adjudicates](/wiki/Adjudication "Adjudication") the validity of [wills](/wiki/Will_(law) "Will (law)"), enforces the provisions of a valid will (by issuing the [grant of probate](/wiki/Probate "Probate")), prevents [malfeasance](/wiki/Malfeasance "Malfeasance") by [executors](/wiki/Executor "Executor") and administrators of [estates](/wiki/Estate_(law) "Estate (law)"), and provides for the [equitable distribution](/wiki/Equitable_distribution "Equitable distribution") of the assets of persons who die [intestate](/wiki/Intestate "Intestate") (without a valid will), such as by granting a [grant of administration](/wiki/Letters_of_Administration "Letters of Administration") giving judicial approval to the [personal representative](/wiki/Personal_representative "Personal representative") to administer matters of the estate.

In contested matters, the probate court examines the authenticity of a will and decides who is to receive the deceased person's property. In a case of an [intestacy](/wiki/Intestacy "Intestacy"), the court determines who is to receive the deceased's property under the law of its jurisdiction. The probate court will then oversee the process of distributing the deceased's assets to the proper beneficiaries. A probate court can be petitioned by interested parties in an estate, such as when a [beneficiary](/wiki/Beneficiary "Beneficiary") feels that an estate is being mishandled. The court has the authority to compel an executor to give an account of their actions.

In some jurisdictions (e.g. [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")) probate courts also handle other matters, such as guardianships, trusts, and mental health issues (including the authority to order involuntary commitment to psychiatric facilities and involuntary administering psychiatric medication). In Texas, there are 18 statutory probate courts[[3]](#cite_note-3) created by legislative statute, called "Probate Courts" which have original and exclusive jurisdiction over its counties probate matters including guardianship cases and mental health commitments. In counties without these probate courts matters are handled by county or district courts.

## Orphans' Court

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Orphans' Court")]

At least in [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland")[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), probate courts are still called orphans' courts, a name that in Maryland dates back to 1777 and reflects "the fact that the children of deceased male landowners were considered orphans when the father died."[[1]](#cite_note-auto-1) The purpose of these courts was to protect against abuses by stepparents and others. Today they hear matters involving contested wills of deceased estates and supervising estates that are probated judicially.[[6]](#cite_note-MarlandOrphCrt-6)

## Register of Probate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Register of Probate")]

A Register of Probate is an elected position in some jurisdictions in the United States, such as New Hampshire,[[7]](#cite_note-nh-7) Massachusetts,[[8]](#cite_note-wgbh-8) and Maine[[9]](#cite_note-washc-9) (part of Massachusetts before 1820). **Register of Wills** is an elected position in jurisdictions such as Maryland.

The Registrar and staff administer the local Probate Court, typically for a given [county](/wiki/County_(United_States) "County (United States)"), acting partly as public customer service and partly as clerks for the probate judge (who may or may not be elected).

## List of probate courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: List of probate courts")]

The following is a partial list of probate courts:

### [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: England and Wales")]

* [Prerogative court](/wiki/Prerogative_court "Prerogative court")—former
* [Court of Probate](/wiki/Court_of_Probate "Court of Probate")—former
* [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice") Family Division—current

### [State courts](/wiki/State_court_(United_States) "State court (United States)") of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: State courts of the United States")]

* [California Superior Court](/wiki/California_Superior_Court "California Superior Court")
* [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut")—[Connecticut Probate Courts](/wiki/Connecticut_Probate_Courts "Connecticut Probate Courts") (a system of 54 probate court districts)
* [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware "Delaware")—Office of [Register of Wills](/w/index.php?title=Register_of_Wills&action=edit&redlink=1 "Register of Wills (page does not exist)")
* [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia")—[Superior Court of the District of Columbia](/wiki/Superior_Court_of_the_District_of_Columbia "Superior Court of the District of Columbia"), Probate Division
* [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") [Florida Circuit Court](/wiki/Florida_Circuit_Court "Florida Circuit Court"), County Comptroller's Office
* [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)")—Probate Court formerly known as the Court of Ordinary (judge formally known as *ordinary*)
* [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland")—County Orphans' Courts,[[6]](#cite_note-MarlandOrphCrt-6) [Office of Register of Wills](https://web.archive.org/web/20130701120718/http://www.princegeorgescountymd.gov/sites/orphanscourt/Pages/default.aspx)
* [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts")—[Probate and Family Court](/wiki/Massachusetts_Probate_and_Family_Court "Massachusetts Probate and Family Court"), Register of Probate
* [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan")—County Probate Courts[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri")—conducted by [Circuit Courts](/wiki/Missouri_Circuit_Courts "Missouri Circuit Courts"), some of which have separate probate divisions, Office of [Public Administrator](/wiki/Public_Administrator "Public Administrator")
* [New Hampshire](/wiki/New_Hampshire "New Hampshire")—[New Hampshire Probate Court](/wiki/New_Hampshire_Probate_Court "New Hampshire Probate Court")
* [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey")—[New Jersey Superior Court](/wiki/New_Jersey_Superior_Court "New Jersey Superior Court"), Chancery Division, Probate Part, [Surrogate's Court](/wiki/Surrogate%27s_Court "Surrogate's Court") (judges known as *surrogates*), Surrogate's Office
* [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)")—[New York Surrogate's Court](/wiki/New_York_Surrogate%27s_Court "New York Surrogate's Court") (judges known as *surrogates*)
* [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio")—conducted by [Courts of Common Pleas](/wiki/Ohio_Courts_of_Common_Pleas "Ohio Courts of Common Pleas"), Family and Probate Divisions, Probate Court
* [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania")—Orphans' Court Division of the Court of Common Pleas,[[11]](#cite_note-11) [Office of Register of Wills](http://www.buckscounty.org/government/RowOfficers/RegisterofWills) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20190215094354/http://www.buckscounty.org/government/rowofficers/RegisterofWills) 2019-02-15 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine")
* [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas")—see [Judiciary of Texas](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Texas "Judiciary of Texas"); the county court handles probate matters in most instances, but its jurisdiction may overlap with the district court. Also, in ten specific counties the [Texas Legislature](/wiki/Texas_Legislature "Texas Legislature") has established one or more Probate Courts to handle probate matters, removing them from county or district court jurisdiction.
* [Vermont](/wiki/Vermont "Vermont")—[Probate Courts](/wiki/Vermont_court_system#Probate_courts "Vermont court system"), one in each of [Vermont's 14 counties](/wiki/Vermont_court_system "Vermont court system")
* [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia")—[Virginia Circuit Court](/wiki/Virginia_Circuit_Court "Virginia Circuit Court")

### [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada")

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Probate_court&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Canada")]

* [New Brunswick](/wiki/New_Brunswick "New Brunswick")—[Probate Court of New Brunswick](/w/index.php?title=Probate_Court_of_New_Brunswick&action=edit&redlink=1 "Probate Court of New Brunswick (page does not exist)")
* [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia")—[Probate Court of Nova Scotia](/w/index.php?title=Probate_Court_of_Nova_Scotia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Probate Court of Nova Scotia (page does not exist)")
* All other provinces are constitutionally required to process probate through their superior courts as per *[section 96](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867#Section_96_courts "Constitution Act, 1867")* of the *[Constitution Act, 1867](/wiki/Constitution_Act,_1867 "Constitution Act, 1867")*.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario")—[Ontario Surrogate's Court](/w/index.php?title=Ontario_Surrogate%27s_Court&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ontario Surrogate's Court (page does not exist)")
